profile.behavior: Social, noisy ground forager that hops and pecks for seeds, grains, insects, and discarded food; often feeds in flocks and nests in cavities or niches in buildings and other structures.
field_marks: Chunky, full-breasted sparrow with a round head, short tail, and stout conical bill.
Male: gray crown, white cheeks, chestnut nape, and a distinct black throat-and-breast bib.
Female and immature: plainer buffy brown and gray, with a pale eyebrow and gray-brown underparts.
Back and wings strongly streaked or patterned in brown, black, and buff; often shows a pale wingbar.
Dark eye; pinkish-brown legs and a short, squared-to-slightly-notched tail.
profile.habitat: Closely associated with human-built environments, including towns, suburbs, farms, parks, and buildings; generally absent from extensive undisturbed woodland, forest, and grassland.

sources.title: House Sparrow Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
House Sparrow Life History — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: No concrete species, anatomy, or text error found. The profile rendering is consistent with one head, one bill, two wings (far wing occluded), two legs, and one short, slightly notched tail.

The male plumage accurately shows gray crown, chestnut nape, white cheeks, black bib, patterned brown-and-buff wings, dark eye, stout conical bill, and pinkish-brown legs; the inset wing and head studies support the identification.

All visible claims match the live field-guide verification, including Passer domesticus, Passeridae, the stated 15–17 cm length, 27–30 g weight, 19–25 cm wingspan, habitat, behavior, and field marks. No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.

verification_sources.title: House Sparrow Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
